docs.title: valkit
docs.text:
Financial statement analysis for AI IDEs and coding agents.

valkit scans a ticker, builds financial ratios from income statements, balance sheets, and cash flows, and returns a deterministic analyst brief that is safe for AI tooling. Just as mustel gives AI IDEs ground truth about code, valkit gives AI IDEs ground truth about financial statements.

pip install valkit

docs.title: Why valkit?
docs.text: Engine What it catches Valuation Overpriced or undervalued relative to sector peers Health Balance sheet stress, leverage, and credit risk Flags Earnings manipulation risk and cash flow trouble

docs.title: The analyst_brief — The Key Innovation
docs.text:
The analyst_brief is a deterministic summary generated from financial metrics and red flags. It is designed for AI IDEs to consume without relying on generative models.

Example:

{
  "analyst_brief": "Strong fundamentals (Piotroski F-score of 7). Financially safe (Altman Z-score of 4.20). Revenue growth is 11.2% over three years and operating margins are stable. No significant red flags detected. Suitable for DCF or comparable company analysis."
}

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
1. Install.
  2. Run valkit scan INFY.
  3. Read the summary, ratios, and red flags.

docs.title: MCP Server
docs.text:
Use valkit as an MCP tool for Claude, Cursor, or any other MCP-aware IDE.

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "valkit": {
      "command": "valkit",
      "args": ["serve"],
      "description": "Financial statement analysis — real ratios for any ticker"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial release
  • valuation, health, growth, flags engines
  • CLI: scan, compare, screen, serve, info, check
  • MCP server with 5 tools
  • Streamlit dashboard
  • MkDocs documentation

docs.title: CLI Reference
docs.text:
Commands:

  • valkit scan TICKER: Run a full scan.
  • valkit scan TICKER --json: Output raw JSON.
  • valkit scan TICKER --export FILE.json: Save scan output.
  • valkit compare TICKER_A TICKER_B: Compare two tickers.
  • valkit screen: Run a simple screener.
  • valkit serve: Start the MCP server.
  • valkit info: Show version and data source.
  • valkit check TICKER: Quick health check.

docs.title: What is MCP?
docs.text: MCP is a protocol for AI tools to call external tools over a structured interface. valkit exposes financial analysis through MCP so that AI IDEs can use real company ratios instead of guessing.

docs.title: Tools
docs.text: Tool Input Output scan ticker Full ScanResult JSON scan_ticker ticker, exchange Full ScanResult JSON compare ticker_a, ticker_b Comparison JSON screen sector, min_piotroski, max_de List of matching scan results info none Version and data source metadata

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Use the same MCP server configuration for Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, or any MCP-aware client.

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "valkit": {
      "command": "valkit",
      "args": ["serve"],
      "description": "Financial statement analysis — real ratios for any ticker"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
Ask the assistant: What is INFY's financial health?

The assistant calls the scan tool and returns a response grounded in real financial metrics.

docs.title: 1. Install
docs.text: pip install valkit

docs.title: 2. Scan your first ticker
docs.text:
valkit scan INFY

You should see a summary table for valuation, health, and growth metrics, followed by an analyst_brief panel.

docs.title: 3. Understand the output
docs.text:
• ticker: the stock symbol.
  • valuation: price multiples and sector comparison.
  • health: Piotroski F-score, Altman Z-score, leverage, and liquidity.
  • growth: three-year revenue and PAT CAGR, margin trend, and free cash flow yield.
  • red_flags: any concerning signals.
  • analyst_brief: deterministic text summarizing strengths and risks.

docs.title: 4. Add to Claude Desktop MCP config
docs.text:
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "valkit": {
      "command": "valkit",
      "args": ["serve"],
      "description": "Financial statement analysis — real ratios for any ticker"
    }
  }
}

docs.title: 5. Ask Claude
docs.text:
Ask: Analyse INFY financials.

Claude will call valkit to fetch real financial metrics and provide a grounded answer instead of hallucinating numbers.

docs.title: SDK
docs.text:
Use the Python SDK to embed valkit in notebooks or automation.

from valkit import scan, compare

result = scan("INFY")
print(result.analyst_brief)

comparison = compare("INFY", "TCS")
print(comparison.result_a.valuation.pe_ratio)

For DataFrame output:

from valkit.sdk.reconciler import compare_to_dataframe

comparison = compare("INFY", "TCS")
df = compare_to_dataframe(comparison)
print(df)

docs.title: Piotroski F-score
docs.text:
The Piotroski F-score is a nine-point score that measures a company's financial strength.

Signal Formula What it measures ROA positive Net Income / Total Assets > 0 Profitability CFO positive Cash flow from operations > 0 Cash generation ROA increasing Current ROA > Prior ROA Improving profitability Accruals Net Income - CFO < 0 Quality of earnings Leverage decreasing D/E current < D/E prior Lower financial risk Liquidity increasing Current ratio current > prior Better short-term coverage No dilution Shares outstanding not increased Shareholder value preservation Gross margin increasing Current gross margin > prior Margin improvement Asset turnover increasing Sales / Assets current > prior Efficiency gain

docs.title: Altman Z-score
docs.text:
The Altman Z-score estimates bankruptcy risk using five factors.

Factor Formula Interpretation X1 (Current Assets - Current Liabilities) / Total Assets Working capital ratio X2 Retained Earnings / Total Assets Cumulative profitability X3 EBIT / Total Assets Operating earnings X4 Market Cap / Total Liabilities Market leverage X5 Revenue / Total Assets Asset efficiency

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Z > 2.99 — Safe zone
  • 1.81 <= Z <= 2.99 — Grey zone
  • Z < 1.81 — Distress zone

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
Healthy company output:

{
  "piotroski_f": 7,
  "altman_z": 4.2,
  "debt_to_equity": 0.08,
  "current_ratio": 2.1,
  "interest_coverage": 48.3
}

Distressed company output:

{
  "piotroski_f": 3,
  "altman_z": 1.5,
  "debt_to_equity": 3.5,
  "current_ratio": 0.8,
  "interest_coverage": 1.2
}
